description:
You are a capacity-planning analyst who has just logged in to a freshly-provisioned Linux workstation (the account is /home/user).  
Your goal is to set up a tiny, self-contained “capacity planner” workspace that demonstrates correct handling of environment variables stored in a dotenv file, and to produce a very small report that an automated tool can later parse.

Complete ALL of the following steps by issuing normal shell commands:

1. Workspace directory  
   • Create a new directory exactly at  
     /home/user/capacity_planner

2. Dotenv file (.env)  
   • Inside /home/user/capacity_planner create a file literally named .env  
   • It must contain precisely **three** key=value pairs, one per line, no extra spaces:  
     CPU_THRESHOLD=75  
     MEM_THRESHOLD=80  
     DISK_THRESHOLD=85  

3. Reporting script  
   • Still inside /home/user/capacity_planner write a Bash script called analyze_usage.sh  
   • The script must:  
     a. Load the variables from the .env file (do **not** hard-code the numbers).  
     b. Emit the report shown in step 4 to STDOUT and also append it to a log file (same text) at  
        /home/user/reports/usage_report.log  
     c. The script itself should be executable.

4. Log file (usage_report.log) – required exact text block  
   • The automated grader will look for the log file at /home/user/reports/usage_report.log  
   • It must contain **exactly one** copy of the block below (you may have extra blank lines before/after it, but no duplicate blocks).  
   • The Timestamp line must use ISO-8601 format (YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS) but the grader ignores the actual number; it only checks that the line starts with “Timestamp: ”.  
   • **Every other line must match character-for-character** (case, spacing, punctuation, trailing spaces).  

===== Capacity Planning Report =====  
Timestamp: <ANY-ISO-8601-DATETIME>  
Thresholds (from .env):  
  CPU: 75%  
  Memory: 80%  
  Disk: 85%  
===== End =====  

5. Verification hint (not graded)  
   After writing the script you can run it once to generate the log file.  
   cat /home/user/reports/usage_report.log should show the block above.

When you are finished there must be:
• the directory /home/user/capacity_planner  
• the dotenv file with the three thresholds  
• an executable Bash script that reads the dotenv file  
• the log file at /home/user/reports/usage_report.log that contains exactly the text block shown.

The grader will fail if:
• any line in the log block is missing or has extra/missing whitespace  
• the .env file has extra keys, different values, or misplaced whitespace  
• the log file or analyze_usage.sh is in the wrong location or missing execute permission.

Do NOT remove or modify anything else on the system.
truth:
Expected artefacts after the agent finishes:

1. /home/user/capacity_planner/.env  
   Contents (exact, with trailing newline at EOF):  
CPU_THRESHOLD=75  
MEM_THRESHOLD=80  
DISK_THRESHOLD=85  

2. /home/user/capacity_planner/analyze_usage.sh  
   • Must be executable (chmod + x)  
   • The actual implementation is not checked, but FILE MUST exist and have shebang #!/bin/bash or #!/usr/bin/env bash.

3. /home/user/reports/usage_report.log  
   • Must exist.  
   • Must contain **exactly one** copy of the following 7 lines (the timestamp value may differ, everything else fixed).  
===== Capacity Planning Report =====  
Timestamp: 20XX-XX-XXTXX:XX:XX  
Thresholds (from .env):  
  CPU: 75%  
  Memory: 80%  
  Disk: 85%  
===== End =====  

   • The grader will:  
     a. Read the file.  
     b. Use regex to locate a block that matches the above, allowing any ISO-8601 datetime after “Timestamp: ”.  
     c. Fail if more than one such block exists or if any of the fixed lines differ (including spacing).

Directory /home/user/reports must exist (the agent creates it) and be writeable by the user.

No other files or directories are checked.
